1.pericope_label: Doing Good for the Right Reasons
1.synthesis: Paul extends the gospel's ethical reach from household to civil life: remind the Cretan believers to be submissive to rulers and authorities, to obey, to be ready for every good work, to speak evil of no one, to avoid quarreling, to be gentle, and to show perfect courtesy to everyone (vv.1–2). The motive for this civic gentleness is not social conformism but theological humility: for we ourselves were once foolish, disobedient, led astray, slaves to various passions and pleasures, passing our days in malice and envy, hated by others and hating one another (v.3). The recollection of what we were is the ground for how we treat what others currently are.
1.historical_context: Gill on being subject to principalities and powers: this refers to men in high places — the Roman emperor and senate, and their subordinate governors in the provinces. The Cretan false teachers, who were insubordinate (1:10) and who spoke evil of dignities, had apparently modeled civic contempt as well as doctrinal heterodoxy. The instruction to be ready for every good work (v.1) carries the same accent as 2:14 (zealous for good works): the gospel produces a community that is pro-active about civic contribution. Gill on remembering our former state: nothing has a greater tendency to promote humility and check pride than to reflect on what we ourselves once were — the slave to passions and the object of hatred that grace found and saved.
1.literary_structure: The three verses form a rhetorical preparation for the gospel statement of vv.4–7. The civic duties of vv.1–2 (submit, obey, do good, speak well, be gentle) are followed by the self-description of v.3 (what we were before grace). The logic is: show these virtues toward the ungodly because you were once ungodly yourself. This grounds the community's civic engagement in the gospel's logic of grace: as God treated us with kindness when we were foolish and disobedient (vv.4–5), so we are to treat the foolish and disobedient with the courtesy and gentleness that reflects that grace. The ethical instruction and the theological gospel are inseparably linked.
1.theological_application: Gill's list of what we once were — foolish, disobedient, deceived, enslaved to passions, malicious, envious, hateful and hated — is the profile of natural humanity before grace. The inclusion of malice and envy alongside the more obvious vices places relational sins on the same level as moral failures: the person who speaks evil of no one (v.2) is not practicing a different morality from what they were saved from but is living out the transformation grace has brought. Matthew Henry observes that the recollection of our former state prevents the self-righteousness that would dismiss the currently unconverted as beyond care: we were where they are, and grace found us; the same grace can find them.
1.christology: The description of our former enslavement (v.3) serves as the dark backdrop against which the appearing of God's kindness and love in Christ (v.4) shines. The catalog of what we were — hateful and hating, malicious and envious — is the condition from which Christ's redemption rescued us. Gill connects the description to the entire arc of the letter: the false teachers of ch.1 who deny God by their works (1:16) are living in what ch.3 describes as the pre-grace state; their problem is not merely doctrinal but ontological — they have not been transformed by the grace that has appeared. The civic virtues Paul calls for in vv.1–2 are possible only for those who have been made new by the grace that follows in vv.4–7.

4.synthesis: Against the dark backdrop of v.3, the gospel breaks in: but when the goodness and loving kindness of God our Savior appeared, he saved us — not because of works done by us in righteousness, but according to his own mercy, by the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Spirit, whom he poured out on us richly through Jesus Christ our Savior, so that being justified by his grace we might become heirs according to the hope of eternal life (vv.4–7). This saying is trustworthy, and Paul insists that Titus affirm it constantly — so that those who have believed in God may be careful to devote themselves to good works (v.8).
4.historical_context: Gill on the goodness and loving kindness of God appearing: the kindness of God is his gentleness, courteous and affable demeanor toward his creatures; the love of man is his philanthropy — his love toward the human race as a species, which moved him to provide a Savior. This appearance parallels the grace of God appearing in 2:11: the same divine movement toward humanity in Christ runs through both passages. Gill on the washing of regeneration: not water baptism, though that is a sign; but the internal laver of the new birth, the Spirit's cleansing work in the heart — compared to water because of its purifying, refreshing, and reviving effects. The pouring out of the Spirit richly through Jesus Christ echoes Acts 2:33 and the Pentecost event as the historical moment when the Spirit was given to regenerate and renew believers.
4.literary_structure: The passage is among Paul's most concentrated soteriological statements. The movement is: appearance of divine character (v.4) → the saving act (v.5a) → the negative ground (not by works, v.5b) → the positive means (washing and renewing by the Spirit, v.5c) → the Spirit's source (Jesus Christ our Savior, v.6) → the legal status (justified by grace, v.7a) → the eschatological status (heirs of eternal life, v.7b) → the practical conclusion (good works, v.8). The entire arc from appearance to heirship is accomplished by God — the human is passive in the grammar of vv.4–7, which is the grammar of grace. The good works of v.8 are then the consequence, not the condition.
4.theological_application: Gill on not by works of righteousness which we have done: the great instance of divine kindness is salvation itself, and it is denied to rest on any human works — even the best, done in the best manner. This is the letter's clearest anti-works statement and the theological bedrock for the catechetical program of ch.2: the good works to which believers are called are not the cause of their salvation but its fruit. Chrysostom on the faithful saying and its pastoral application: having spoken of the love of God to man and of what he has done for us, Paul adds: therefore those who believe should be careful to maintain good works. The soteriological indicative (vv.4–7) drives the ethical imperative (v.8): because of what God has done, live accordingly.
4.christology: The Spirit is poured out richly through Jesus Christ our Savior (v.6) — Christ is the channel of the Spirit's regenerating and renewing work. The justification by his grace in v.7 identifies the grace as Christ's own grace: it is by what he accomplished that the status of heir is conferred. The two appearances of the divine Savior — goodness and kindness appearing (v.4) and the literal appearing of Christ in 2:11 and 2:13 — are one event: the incarnation that brought grace into history, accomplished the ransom (2:14), and poured out the Spirit that regenerates and renews. The heirs of eternal life are heirs through Christ, by the Spirit, to the Father who loved them from before the ages began (1:2).

9.synthesis: Paul instructs Titus to avoid foolish controversies, genealogies, dissensions, and quarrels about the law, for they are unprofitable and worthless (v.9). As for a person who stirs up division — after warning him once and then twice, have nothing more to do with him, knowing that such a person is warped and sinful, self-condemned (vv.10–11). The three-verse section is the negative complement to v.8's positive call to good works: as Titus is to affirm the gospel and promote good works, so he is to avoid fruitless speculative debate and discipline those who foster it.
9.historical_context: Gill on foolish questions: those started in the schools of the Jews, concerning the ages of the world, the state of the soul after death, or the nature of angels — things that are curious, rather than profitable. Matthew Henry connects the genealogies and law-quarrels to the circumcision party's debates from ch.1: the same opponents who upset households with Jewish myths now demand disputational engagement with their legal speculations. Gill on the heretic: one who, by the notation of the word, makes his own opinion rather than the received faith of the churches his standard, separates from them, forms a party, and sets himself at its head. The first and second warning before withdrawal mirrors the church discipline principle of Matthew 18:15–17: patient, staged correction before exclusion.
9.literary_structure: The unit has two movements: what to avoid (v.9) and how to handle those who persist in it (vv.10–11). The avoidance instruction is directed at Titus himself — he is not to be drawn into the disputational orbit of the false teachers. The discipline instruction addresses those who stir up division: the sequence (one warning, then two, then withdrawal) assumes good faith correction attempts before the community's separation. The self-condemned verdict of v.11 is striking: the heretic's own conscience already witnesses against him, making the community's withdrawal a recognition of what he has already done to himself rather than an external punishment.
9.theological_application: Gill on the heretic being subverted and self-condemned: he is like an edifice entirely demolished, with no hope of restoration. He is in a desperate condition — having opposed Christ's person or sacrifice or the Spirit's grace, and having erred from the truth knowingly. The two-warning process reflects the pastoral patience Paul has modeled throughout the Pastorals: the goal is restoration (sharply rebuke so that they may be sound in the faith, 1:13), and only when restoration is persistently refused does separation follow. The worthlessness of the controversies is not merely pragmatic (they waste time) but theological (they displace the trustworthy saying of v.8 and the gospel it contains with human speculation).
9.christology: The foolish controversies and genealogies to be avoided are diversions from the christological center of the trustworthy word (v.8). The law-quarrels of the Cretan opponents are ultimately disputes about how to be right with God — a question the gospel has answered in Christ: justified by his grace, heirs through him. The heretic who persists in dividing the community is resisting not merely an ecclesiastical structure but the community constituted by the grace that appeared in Christ. Separation from such a person is not a failure of mercy but a protection of the community whose life together is built on the appearing of grace rather than on speculative achievement.

12.synthesis: Paul closes with personal logistics: when he sends Artemas or Tychicus, Titus should come to him at Nicopolis, where Paul has decided to spend the winter (v.12). He is to help Zenas the lawyer and Apollos on their way with everything they need (v.13). And let the believers also learn to devote themselves to good works, so as to help in cases of urgent need — so that they not be unfruitful (v.14). All who are with Paul greet Titus; greet those who love them in the faith. Grace be with you all (v.15).
12.historical_context: Gill on Artemas: there is no other mention of him; some traditions place him among the seventy disciples and later bishop of Lystra, but these are uncertain. Tychicus is better attested — a frequent Pauline messenger, also sent to Ephesus and Colossae. Nicopolis (City of Victory) was on the western coast of Greece, likely Actia Nicopolis; Paul's decision to winter there suggests active mission planning rather than retreat. Gill on Zenas the lawyer: either trained in Greek or Roman civil law, or a Jewish scribe and interpreter of Moses's law. Apollos, the Alexandrian preacher familiar from 1 Corinthians 3:6, is apparently on a journey through Crete. The instruction to help them generously (v.13) models the good works that have been the letter's theme throughout.
12.literary_structure: The personal close performs the letter's theology: Titus is embedded in a web of relationships and mobile ministry — messengers coming, workers traveling, churches hosting. The instruction of v.14 — let ours also learn to devote themselves to good works — brings the letter's central theme (good works as the fruit of grace) into the conclusion. The word unfruitful echoes the worthlessness of the controversies in v.9: the alternative to being fruitful in good works is being unfruitful in vain disputes. Grace be with you all closes the letter with the same note that grounded the entire instruction: grace is the letter's first and last word.
12.theological_application: Gill on let ours learn to maintain good works: this may refer specifically to the Cretan believers being taught by the itinerant teachers (Zenas and Apollos need support — let the community's good works be expressed in tangible provision). Barnes connects maintaining good works to the honest trades and occupations that provide both for personal needs and for ministry support. The recurring motif of good works throughout Titus — introduced in 1:16 as what the false teachers are unfit for, called for in 2:7 and 2:14 as the fruit of grace and redemption, insisted on in 3:8 as the practical expression of believing in God, and concluded in 3:14 as the community's responsibility — shows that the entire letter is organized around the claim that true doctrine produces a community of visible good works.
12.christology: The grace be with you all that closes the letter is not a casual formula but the letter's theological conclusion: all that Paul has commanded — the elder qualifications, the catechetical program, the gospel of appearing and redemption and justification, the avoidance of fruitless controversy, the devotion to good works — is possible only by the grace that has appeared in Christ (2:11). The Christ who gave himself for us to redeem us and purify a people zealous for good works (2:14) is the source of the grace that closes the letter. Every good work the Cretan believers are called to do flows from the appearing of that grace, is empowered by the poured-out Spirit (3:6), and awaits the blessed hope of his return (2:13).
